title: Ancient Legged Snake Rewrites Evolutionary History
summary: A well-preserved fossil of the ancient snake Najash rionegrina, discovered in Argentina, provides new insights into snake evolution. This 100-million-year-old specimen had hind legs and a cheekbone (jugal bone), a feature lost in modern snakes. The study, published in Science Advances in 2019, used micro-CT scanning to reveal detailed skull anatomy, including nerve and blood vessel paths. The findings challenge the older hypothesis that snakes evolved from small burrowing ancestors. Instead, evidence suggests the ancestors of modern snakes were larger-bodied with wide mouths. The research also shows that early snakes retained their hindlimbs for an extended period before the evolution of limbless modern snakes. The study corrected a 160-year-old misunderstanding about the jugal bone in snakes. Later studies, including a 2020 discovery of a blind snake from Brazil and a 2023 brain reconstruction study, further support the idea that early snake evolution in Gondwana was diverse and often involved larger body sizes. A 2025 study on a Jurassic squamate from Scotland added more complexity, indicating that early squamate evolution involved anatomical experimentation and convergent evolution.

the process by which species change over time through genetic variation and natural selection
cheekbone; a bone in the skull that is present in many reptiles but lost in modern snakes
a technique using X-rays to create detailed 3D images of the internal structure of objects
an ancient supercontinent that included present-day South America, Africa, Australia, and Antarctica

background_read: Snakes evolved from legged lizards over millions of years. Fossils like Najash provide crucial evidence of transitional forms. The jugal bone is a key feature in understanding skull evolution. Gondwana was a supercontinent that broke apart, and many ancient snake fossils are found in former Gondwanan landmasses.
Article_Structure: Main Points: The Najash fossil shows early snakes had hind legs and a jugal bone, challenging the burrowing ancestor hypothesis; evidence suggests large-bodied ancestors. Purpose: To clarify snake evolution and correct long-standing anatomical misunderstandings. Evidence Evaluation: The study uses high-resolution micro-CT scans, providing empirical data; the fossil is well-preserved. Author Credibility: Lead author Fernando Garberoglio from Fundación Azara at Universidad Maimónides; co-author Michael Caldwell, a professor with expertise in paleontology. Methodology: Micro-CT scanning to reconstruct skull details without damaging the fossil; comparative anatomy with modern and other fossil snakes.

perspectives.description: View the discovery as a major step in understanding snake evolution, correcting previous misconceptions about the jugal bone.
See it as evidence for complex evolutionary pathways and the importance of fossil evidence in testing hypotheses.
